chunk_summary: This section outlines age- and puberty-specific metabolic screening bundles for children, emphasizing risk-based testing for obesity, dyslipidemia, glucose metabolism, and liver function, with tailored recommendations for different age groups and risk factors.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of age and pubertal stage in determining metabolic screening protocols for children.
Identify key metabolic risk factors and appropriate screening tests for children aged 2–9 years and ≥10 years.
Learn the recommended frequency and components of metabolic screening bundles for children with obesity or additional risk factors.
key_facts: Children aged 2–9 years with obesity or high central adiposity should be screened for BP, waist trends, and lipids; dyslipidemia evaluation is recommended for severe obesity.
In Indian settings, ALT screening is advised earlier in cases of severe obesity, acanthosis, or multiple metabolic abnormalities.
For children ≥10 years or at pubertal onset, core screening includes dyslipidemia, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and liver function tests (ALT ± AST).
The ADA recommends diabetes screening for youth ≥10 years or post-puberty with overweight/obesity and at least one additional risk factor, repeating tests every 3 years if normal.
Minimum screening bundle includes fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, ALT, and BP; preferred bundle adds HbA1c, urine dipstick, and expanded liver panel.
reference_quote: The American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee 'Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026' supports risk-based diabetes screening after puberty onset or age 10 in youth with overweight/obesity plus at least one additional risk factor.
document_key_topics: Risk-based screening for obesity and related conditions
Screening bundles for dyslipidemia, glucose metabolism, and liver function
